However, a persistent challenge within CLIP is the modality gap between its vision and text embeddings, a structural separation within the embedding space that lim- its the effectiveness of cross-modal alignment [17, 45]. Abstract The modality gap between vision and text embeddings in CLIP presents a significant challenge for zero-shot im- age captioning, limiting effective cross-modal representa- tion. In this paper, we introduce Diffusion Bridge, an inno- vative approach that leverages diffusion models to directly address the modality gap within CLIP’s embedding space for zero-shot image captioning. Experimental results demonstrate that these text- like vision embeddings significantly enhance alignment with their paired text embeddings, leading to improved zero-shot captioning performance on MSCOCO and Flickr30K.

ing a powerful joint embedding space for visual and tex-
tual data, which has paved the way for advancements in
cross-modal tasks [6, 13, 26, 26, 38, 40]. With its powerful

Among these applications, zero- shot image captioning stands out, where models are re- quired to generate descriptive captions for images without having seen any paired image-caption data during training [7, 15, 28, 35, 43].
At the core of these advancements is Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM) [10], which iteratively refine noisy vectors to capture the intrinsic data distribution with high fidelity.
By training the diffusion model on text embeddings, the model learns the distributional characteristics of the text embedding space.
CLIP’s inherent cross-modal alignment has made it a central model for such endeavors.

Conclusion Diffusion Bridge introduces an innovative solution to the modality gap in CLIP-based zero-shot image captioning by leveraging diffusion models to bring vision embed- dings into closer alignment with the text embedding space.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
The modality gap between vision and text embeddings in
CLIP presents a significant challenge for zero-shot im-
age captioning, limiting effective cross-modal representa-
tion. Traditional approaches, such as noise injection and
memory-based similarity matching, attempt to address this
gap, yet these methods either rely on indirect alignment or
relatively naive solutions with heavy computation. Diffu-
sion Bridge introduces a novel approach to directly reduce
this modality gap by leveraging Denoising Diffusion Prob-
abilistic Models (DDPM), trained exclusively on text em-
beddings to model their distribution. Our approach is mo-
tivated by the observation that, while paired vision and text
embeddings are relatively close, a modality gap still ex-
ists due to stable regions created by the contrastive loss.
This gap can be interpreted as noise in cross-modal map-
pings, which we approximate as Gaussian noise. To bridge
this gap, we employ a reverse diffusion process, where im-
age embeddings are strategically introduced at an inter-
mediate step in the reverse process, allowing them to be
refined progressively toward the text embedding distribu-
tion. This process transforms vision embeddings into text-
like representations closely aligned with paired text embed-
dings, effectively minimizing discrepancies between modal-
ities. Experimental results demonstrate that these text-
like vision embeddings significantly enhance alignment with
their paired text embeddings, leading to improved zero-shot
captioning performance on MSCOCO and Flickr30K. Dif-
fusion Bridge achieves competitive results without reliance
on memory banks or entity-driven methods, offering a novel
pathway for cross-modal alignment and opening new pos-
sibilities for the application of diffusion models in multi-
modal tasks. The source code is available at: https:
//github.com/mongeoroo/diffusion-bridge
*Corresponding author.
Figure 1. Overview of Diffusion Bridge for zero-shot image cap-
tioning. Diffusion Bridge utilizes a diffusion model trained on text
embeddings to bridge the modality gap in CLIP. During inference,
vision embeddings are introduced into the reverse diffusion pro-
cess at an intermediate timestep, progressively aligned with the
text embedding space. This alignment facilitates improved zero-
shot image captioning performance by producing embeddings that
closely match the text distribution
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
Large-scale pre-trained vision-language models have gar-
nered significant attention in recent research, demonstrating
impressive performance across various multi-modal tasks
[3, 8, 11, 31]. Among these models, CLIP [31] (Con-
trastive Language-Image Pretraining) stands out, leverag-
ing a powerful joint embedding space for visual and tex-
tual data, which has paved the way for advancements in
cross-modal tasks [6, 13, 26, 26, 38, 40]. With its powerful
This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;
the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.
4050

pretraining on a large-scale, diverse set of image-text pairs,
CLIP enables impressive zero-shot performance across nu-
merous downstream tasks, from classification to complex
retrieval tasks [23, 35, 42]. Among these applications, zero-
shot image captioning stands out, where models are re-
quired to generate descriptive captions for images without
having seen any paired image-caption data during training
[7, 15, 28, 35, 43]. In zero-shot captioning, a model’s ability
to bridge visual content to meaningful language representa-
tions is critical. CLIP’s inherent cross-modal alignment has
made it a central model for such endeavors.
However, a persistent challenge within CLIP is the
modality gap between its vision and text embeddings, a
structural separation within the embedding space that lim-
its the effectiveness of cross-modal alignment [17, 45]. This
gap, caused by the contrastive learning dynamics and model
initialization, results in visual and textual representations
occupying distinct regions within the shared embedding
space. Consequently, this gap restricts direct transfer and
alignment for zero-shot captioning, where precise semantic
alignment is essential for high-quality caption generation.
Previous zero-shot image captioning methods have ad-
dressed the modality gap challenge through various tech-
niques. Notably, noise injection methods [28, 45] seek
to enhance the decoder’s robustness by introducing Gaus-
sian noise into text embeddings during training, thereby
promoting generalization across the inherent discrepancies
between vision and text embeddings. However, these ap-
proaches do not fundamentally align vision and text embed-
dings, leaving modality mismatches that can hinder zero-
shot performance.
On the other hand, recent advancements in diffusion-
based models have shown considerable promise within
multi-modal domains, particularly in tasks such as image
synthesis and text-guided image manipulation [5, 21, 27].
At the core of these advancements is Denoising Diffusion
Probabilistic Models (DDPM) [10], which iteratively refine
noisy vectors to capture the intrinsic data distribution with
high fidelity. This ability to model complex, nuanced dis-
tributions renders diffusion models powerful tools for novel
applications across a range of multi-modal settings. How-
ever, their application in bridging the modality gap for zero-
shot image captioning remains an area yet to be thoroughly
explored.
In this paper, we introduce Diffusion Bridge, an inno-
vative approach that leverages diffusion models to directly
address the modality gap within CLIP’s embedding space
for zero-shot image captioning. Diffusion Bridge models
the distribution of text embeddings through the diffusion
model, effectively capturing the intricate structure of the
text embedding space. Given that paired vision and text
embeddings remain relatively close despite their inherent
differences, our method strategically inserts vision embed-
dings at an intermediate step of the diffusion reverse pro-
cess. The gradual refinement of reverse process allows
vision embeddings to transform into text-like representa-
tions, incrementally reducing residual discrepancies stem-
ming from the intrinsic characteristics of each modality.
Through comprehensive experiments, we demonstrate
that Diffusion Bridge significantly reduces the modality
gap, resulting in enhanced cross-modal similarity and im-
proved captioning performance. Importantly, Diffusion
Bridge achieves robust zero-shot captioning without re-
liance on large memory banks or external knowledge mod-
ules. Our findings indicate that Diffusion Bridge not only
advances zero-shot captioning but also opens novel poten-
tials for the application of diffusion models in cross-modal
alignment tasks.
raw_sections.method:
3. Method
Diffusion Bridge presents an innovative solution to address
the modality gap by utilizing only text embeddings. Our
approach is motivated by the analysis in C3 [45], which re-
veals that even paired vision and text embeddings, while
close, still exhibit a modality gap. This gap arises due to
stable regions produced by the contrastive loss, resulting in
a constant offset, or ‘alignment noise’, between embeddings
from different modalities. This alignment noise persists in
the shared embedding space and can be approximated as
Gaussian noise.
Our proposed method employs the diffusion model’s
dual strengths: its capacity as a generative model to learn
the intrinsic distribution of text embeddings and its ability
to incorporate conditional inputs during the reverse process.
By training the diffusion model on text embeddings, the
model learns the distributional characteristics of the text
embedding space. During inference, we initiate the reverse
diffusion process with vision embeddings at an intermediate
step. Guided by the learned text distribution, the diffusion
model progressively refines these vision embeddings into
text-like representations. This conditioned reverse map-
ping effectively minimizes residual discrepancies, aligning
vision embeddings more closely with the text embedding
space and thereby mitigating the modality gap.
In the following sections, we describe the mechanisms
and architecture of Diffusion Bridge, detailing each compo-
nent’s role in enabling efficient and scalable zero-shot cap-
tioning. Fig. 2 visualizes the overall pipeline of Diffusion
Bridge.
raw_sections.experiments:
4. Experiments
In this section, we evaluate the effectiveness of Diffusion
Bridge in mitigating the modality gap for zero-shot image
captioning. Our experiments aim to assess how well our
method aligns vision embeddings with the text embedding
space. We conduct evaluations on standard image caption-
ing datasets under both in-domain and cross-domain setups
to comprehensively test the generalization capabilities of
Diffusion Bridge.
Datasets and Metrics: We assess Diffusion Bridge on
two widely used image captioning datasets: MSCOCO
[4, 19] and Flickr30K [39]. For dataset partitioning, we
utilize the standard Karpathy split [12], which is commonly
adopted in captioning benchmarks. For quantitative evalua-
tion, we employ a suite of established metrics to gauge cap-
tion quality, including BLEU (B@n) [29], METEOR (M)
[2], ROUGE-L (R-L) [18], CIDEr (C) [37], and SPICE (S)
[1].
Implementation details: For the DDPM training, we
employ a 1-D UNet architecture with four layers, where
each layer successively doubles in dimensionality. The
model is trained using a batch size of 64, a learning rate
of 8 × 10−5, and a total of 3, 000, 000 steps. The number
of diffusion timesteps is set to T = 1000 and we set C
in Eqt. 6 as 5 in all experiments. For experiments on the
MSCOCO dataset, we set the intermediate and augmenta-
tion steps to M = 600 and A = 400, respectively, whereas
for Flickr30K, these values are configured to M = 400 and
A = 300 . For decoder training, we use CLIP-ViT-B/32
[31] as the encoding backbone and adopt GPT-2 [30] as
the base decoder. The prefix projector consists of two fully
connected layers with a hidden dimensionality of 768. The
learnable soft prompts are defined with a length of 10. The
projection layer projects the CLIP embedding from 512 to
768 dimensions. Training is conducted for 10 epochs with
a batch size of 40 and a learning rate of2 × 10−5, using the
AdamW optimizer following the setup in [28, 45].
4054

Figure 3. UMAP visualization of COCO validation set embed-
dings. Red points represent image embeddings ˆ eimg, blue points
denote text embeddings ˆ etext, and green points show mapped em-
beddings ˜ eimg produced by Diffusion Bridge from image embed-
dings.
raw_sections.conclusion:
5. Conclusion
Diffusion Bridge introduces an innovative solution to the
modality gap in CLIP-based zero-shot image captioning
by leveraging diffusion models to bring vision embed-
dings into closer alignment with the text embedding space.
Through the integration of diffusion-bridging and diffusion-
augmented text embeddings, our approach effectively re-
duces the modality gap, achieving strong performance
across multiple captioning metrics without reliance on noise
injection, memory-intensive methods, or entity-driven hard
prompts. Ablation studies further emphasize the signifi-
cance of each component in Diffusion Bridge and interme-
diate diffusion steps in enhancing robustness and alignment
precision. Our work opens up new directions for research
and practical applications in cross-modal understanding.
